CSS Current Affairs · 19 July class

Contemporary Nuclear Issues & Deterrence in South Asia

The whole contemporary nuclear file in one class: global modernization and the new arms race, Iran and the IAEA, the new technologies, NATO nuclear sharing, Israeli ambiguity, limited war in South Asia, Gulf diplomacy, and China's expansion.

Every topic here ends the same way: what it means for Pakistan. That panel is where the marks are. ☢️

2h 50m class 8 topics 12,241 warheads 9 nuclear states
Contents

🗺️ In this class

  2. 01 · Global modernization & the new arms racethe numbers
  3. 02 · Iran & IAEA safeguardsJCPOA · 60%
  4. 03 · Missile defence, MIRVs & hypersonicsthe technology
  5. 04 · Extended deterrence & NATO sharingthe umbrella

Start here

⭐ The five-minute version

  • The danger has changed shape. The headline of the class: the risk today lies not in the sheer number of weapons but in rapid modernization, weak arms control and rising mistrust. Global warheads are around 12,241, and all nine nuclear states are modernising.
  • New technology is destabilising deterrence. Missile defence, MIRVs and hypersonic weapons shorten decision time, weaken confidence in second-strike capability, and make crises harder to control.
  • Iran is a test of the whole non-proliferation system. After the US left the JCPOA in 2018, Iran reduced compliance, and 60% enrichment sits far above civilian need. The real issue is verification and intent, not just technology.
  • The nuclear order is visibly uneven. NATO nuclear sharing forward-deploys US weapons in Europe while the alliance condemns proliferation, and Israel's ambiguity keeps an undeclared arsenal outside the NPT. Both feed the double-standards argument Pakistan can use.
  • South Asia is the sharpest case, and the one to master. The stability-instability paradox means nuclear weapons deter total war but open space for limited conflict. India seeks room for punitive action below Pakistan's nuclear threshold; Pakistan answers with full-spectrum deterrence to deny that space.
  • The danger is not a deliberate nuclear war but the belief that a limited conventional clash can be controlled, when Kargil 1999 through Balakot 2019 and the May 2025 crisis show how fast events escalate.
  • China has changed the structure. Past 600 warheads and adding roughly 100 a year, it turns deterrence from a US-Russia bilateral problem into a three-power one, which in turn drives India, which in turn drives Pakistan.
  • How to answer any of it. Define the concept, give the global picture, then land on the Pakistan relevance. Every infographic in this class is built with that final panel, and so should your answer be.
1 The global picture

🚀 Global modernization & the new arms race

topic 1

Nuclear modernization means upgrading arsenals, not simply increasing numbers: modernising missiles, submarines, bombers and warheads, improving command and control, early warning, cyber resilience and space surveillance, all aimed at credibility, survivability, speed and precision.

Why a new arms race is emerging

  • Great-power rivalry: US, Russia and China competition.
  • Weak arms control: collapse and erosion of treaties and limits.
  • New technologies: AI, cyber, missile defence, MIRVs, hypersonics.
  • Regional rivalries: South Asia, the Korean Peninsula, Europe, the Middle East.

The figures to quote (January 2025, from SIPRI Yearbook 2025, FAS and ICAN):

  • 12,241 estimated global nuclear warheads
  • 9,614 warheads in military stockpiles
  • 3,912 warheads deployed with missiles and aircraft
  • US and Russia hold the overwhelming majority
  • All 9 nuclear-armed states are modernising
  • $100 billion+ spent on nuclear weapons in 2024, up about 11% on 2023

The nine actors and their trends

StateModernization trend
United StatesModernising the nuclear triad to maintain credibility against Russia and China
RussiaNuclear weapons central to strategy; modernization and new systems continue
ChinaRapid expansion and modernization; arsenal growing fast
IndiaModernising in the context of both Pakistan and China
PakistanIndia-centric deterrence; slow, steady modernization and system improvement
North KoreaNuclear weapons for regime survival and bargaining; no denuclearization
United KingdomMaintaining a credible, independent deterrent; advanced modernization
FranceModernising nuclear forces in response to the security environment
IsraelNuclear ambiguity continues; advanced capabilities

Risks, South Asia, and the way forward

Major risks: action-reaction cycles, crisis instability and miscalculation, shorter decision time from new technology, and weaker disarmament and NPT credibility. In South Asia: India-Pakistan deterrence remains central, China's rise shapes India's posture, Pakistan responds to India's modernization, and missile defence, MIRVs and sea-based systems complicate stability. The way forward: revive arms control frameworks, promote transparency and confidence-building, and invest in crisis-management mechanisms.

The key takeaway, quotable as it stands: the danger today lies not only in the number of weapons, but in rapid modernization, weak arms control, and rising mistrust. That sentence works as the opening or closing line of almost any nuclear essay.

2 The test case

⚛️ Iran & IAEA safeguards

topic 2

The issue in one frame: Iran claims a right to peaceful nuclear energy under the NPT; critics fear the programme could move toward weapons capability. The core problem is trust, transparency and verification.

YearEvent
2015JCPOA nuclear deal agreed
2018United States withdraws from the JCPOA
2019 to 2021Iran gradually reduces compliance
2026IAEA monitoring and diplomatic tensions remain central

Why 60% enrichment matters

  • 60% enriched uranium is far above normal civilian fuel levels.
  • It is much closer to weapons-grade than standard power-reactor fuel.
  • The E3 say Iran is the only non-nuclear-weapon state producing 60% HEU.
  • The issue is not just technology, but political intention and verification.

What IAEA safeguards actually are. Inspection and verification measures whose purpose is to ensure nuclear material is used for peaceful activities. Monitoring depends on access, declarations and cooperation; reduced transparency makes verification harder; and without safeguards, diplomacy becomes far less credible.

Actors, stakes and outcomes

The actors: Iran (insists on peaceful rights and sanctions relief), the IAEA (verifies, inspects, reports), the United States (seeks to prevent weapons capability), the E3 of UK, France and Germany (support diplomacy but demand transparency), and Israel (sees the programme as a direct security threat).

Why it matters: it tests the credibility of the NPT, raises Middle East proliferation risk, deepens US-Iran and Israel-Iran tensions, affects global energy markets, and shows how failed diplomacy weakens non-proliferation.

Four possible outcomes: a diplomatic deal with stronger verification; a frozen crisis with no progress but no major escalation; military escalation through strikes and counter-strikes; or threshold nuclear status, with Iran crossing the nuclear threshold.

Pakistan relevance. Iran is Pakistan's neighbour, so instability has direct regional effects. Gulf tensions move oil prices, trade and Pakistan's economy. Pakistan must balance ties with Iran, the Gulf states, the US and China, and the crisis bears on border security, diplomacy and strategic stability. The slide's own verdict: the best long-term solution remains diplomacy plus credible verification.

3 The technology

🛰️ Missile defence, MIRVs & hypersonics

topic 3
TechnologyWhat it isWhy it destabilises
Missile defenceSystems that detect, track, intercept or destroy incoming missilesDefensive in design, but it weakens an opponent's confidence in retaliation and can trigger action-reaction cycles
MIRVsMultiple Independently Targetable Re-entry Vehicles: one missile carrying several warheads aimed at different targetsIncreases destructive potential and complicates missile defence
Hypersonic weaponsExtremely high-speed delivery systems, harder to track and interceptReduce warning time, increase crisis ambiguity, and raise escalation pressure

Why they matter for strategic stability. They can weaken confidence in second-strike capability, shorten decision-making time in a crisis, encourage arms racing and mistrust, and make deterrence more fragile. The global risks follow: crisis instability and miscalculation, first-strike fears and counterforce concerns, harder arms control and verification, and a technology race in the new nuclear age.

South Asia and Pakistan. India-Pakistan deterrence is sensitive to technological change: India's MIRV and missile-defence developments affect Pakistan's threat perception, the China-India-Pakistan triangle complicates the balance, and hypersonics compress response time. Pakistan's stated line is to preserve credible deterrence without entering an unlimited arms race, focusing on survivability, command-and-control safety, cyber resilience and crisis management. The takeaway: these systems may strengthen deterrence in theory, but in practice they deepen mistrust and accelerate arms racing.

4 The umbrella

🛡️ Extended deterrence & NATO nuclear sharing

topic 4

Extended deterrence is a nuclear-armed state extending protection to its allies. In NATO, the US nuclear umbrella covers European allies, with the purpose of preserving peace, preventing coercion and deterring aggression.

What NATO nuclear sharing is. US nuclear weapons are forward-deployed in Europe, selected allies participate in planning and delivery arrangements, and it works as both a military arrangement and a political symbol of alliance commitment. Independent estimates often place about 100 US B61 bombs in Europe.

Why NATO supports itKey criticisms
Reassures allies close to RussiaKeeps nuclear weapons politically relevant
Deters coercion and aggressionWeakens disarmament norms
Strengthens alliance cohesionSeen as conflicting with the spirit of the NPT
Reduces incentives for allies to build their own weaponsCan increase escalation risk in crises, and reinforces nuclear dependence in Europe

Why it matters now: the Russia-Ukraine war revived European nuclear anxiety, Russian nuclear signalling sharpened deterrence debates, NATO continues modernization and reassurance, and Europe is debating long-term dependence on the US umbrella. Russia's view: Moscow calls nuclear sharing hostile, its nuclear relationship with Belarus intensified tensions, and the classic security dilemma applies, since NATO says deterrence where Russia sees provocation.

Pakistan and CSS relevance. It shows nuclear weapons are tied to alliances and credibility, highlights double standards in global nuclear politics, and offers a useful contrast with South Asia's self-reliant model: Pakistan sits under nobody's umbrella and maintains its own deterrent. Strong material for essays on the NPT, deterrence, alliances and strategic stability.

5 The undeclared arsenal

🕶️ Israel's nuclear ambiguity

topic 5

Nuclear ambiguity, sometimes called opacity, means Israel neither officially confirms nor denies nuclear weapons. It is often linked to the formula that it will not be the first to introduce nuclear weapons in the Middle East. The purpose is to maintain uncertainty while preserving deterrence.

Why adopt it? It gains deterrence benefits without formal declaration, avoids stronger pressure in the debate over joining the NPT as a nuclear-weapon state, and reduces diplomatic costs while keeping the strategic advantage.

The NPT link, and the double standard. Israel sits outside the NPT framework. Its opacity fuels the debate over nuclear double standards, and critics contrast the strict scrutiny applied to Iran with the silence over Israel's undeclared capability. That contrast is the single most useful thing in this topic for a Pakistani candidate.

Why it matters for the region: it shapes threat perceptions, complicates arms control and trust-building, weakens the credibility of the regional non-proliferation order, and makes consensus on a stable nuclear order harder. On Iran: Israel strongly opposes Iranian weapons capability, while Iran and others point to Israeli opacity as a legitimacy issue, showing the political unevenness of regional nuclear debate.

The Middle East Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone. A longstanding regional goal. Arab states and Iran generally want Israel's nuclear status addressed; Israel argues that peace and security guarantees must come first. The core dispute is simply disarmament first or peace first.

The takeaway. Israeli ambiguity may strengthen deterrence for Israel, but it complicates Middle Eastern arms control, weakens non-proliferation credibility, and keeps regional security trapped in mistrust. For CSS it is prime material on non-discriminatory nuclear governance.

6 The core topic

⚔️ Limited war under nuclear conditions in South Asia

topic 6

Limited war means a restricted conflict between nuclear-armed states, limited in objectives, geography, duration and weapons used, fought below the nuclear threshold while avoiding all-out war.

The stability-instability paradox, the concept at the heart of the topic. Nuclear weapons may prevent total war, but in doing so they create space for limited or sub-conventional conflict. Higher-level stability produces lower-level instability. The core idea in one line: total war is deterred, limited conflict is not fully prevented.

India's search for limited war spacePakistan's response
Preference for punitive but controlled conventional responsesPakistan seeks to deny space for limited war
Often linked with surgical strikes, air strikes and limited objectivesFull-spectrum deterrence aims to deter even limited aggression
Seeks room for action below Pakistan's nuclear thresholdSignals that escalation may not stay controllable
Limitation by time, geography and scaleFocus on preserving credible deterrence against conventional pressure

The crisis record

The class lists the South Asian crisis sequence, and these dates are worth memorising: Kargil 1999, the Parliament crisis 2001 to 2002, Mumbai 2008, Uri 2016, Balakot 2019, and the May 2025 crisis. Geography and proximity make escalation faster, both sides may define "limited" differently, and crisis communication and restraint are essential.

The major risks: miscalculation and misperception, rapid escalation during crisis response, domestic politics and media pressure, short decision-making timelines, and drones, missiles and new technologies compressing warning time.

The key takeaway, and the best single quote in the class: the central danger in South Asia is not necessarily deliberate nuclear war, but the belief that a limited conventional conflict can be controlled until events, perceptions and pressures push escalation further. Open or close a South Asia deterrence answer with that and you have framed the entire question.

7 Pakistan's stake

🛢️ Nuclear diplomacy in the Gulf

topic 7

Gulf nuclear diplomacy covers Iran's programme, Saudi nuclear ambitions, Gulf security and safeguards. It involves the US, the Gulf states, Iran, Israel, China, Russia and the IAEA, and it links energy, security, non-proliferation and regional rivalry in one file.

StrandThe substance
IranInsists on a right to peaceful nuclear technology under the NPT; Gulf states view its enrichment and regional posture through a security lens; diplomacy turns on safeguards, transparency and escalation risk.
Saudi ArabiaSeeks civilian nuclear energy as part of economic diversification. Debate centres on safeguards, enrichment and reprocessing restrictions, and regional rivalry makes it politically sensitive.
US-Saudi dealCooperation depends on strict non-proliferation conditions. The core issue: how to enable peaceful energy without future proliferation risk.
The UAE modelOften presented as the regional peaceful nuclear-energy model, where strong safeguards and transparency built international confidence.

Why it matters for Pakistan specifically. Iran is a neighbour, so escalation has direct regional effects. Gulf stability underpins remittances, trade and energy security. Tension in the Strait of Hormuz raises oil prices and hurts the economy. And Pakistan must balance ties with Iran and the Gulf Arab partners at the same time.

The risks: oil-price shocks, diplomatic pressure to take sides, sectarian spillover and polarisation, diaspora vulnerability in the Gulf states, and wider proliferation pressure if rivalry intensifies.

Pakistan's best approach, straight off the slide and usable as a policy paragraph: support de-escalation, dialogue and regional stability; back peaceful nuclear use under safeguards; maintain balanced diplomacy with Iran, Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Qatar; present Pakistan as a responsible nuclear state; and avoid becoming a party to Gulf rivalries.

8 The structural shift

🐉 China & the emerging three-power order

topic 8

China's expansion means rapid growth and modernization of its nuclear forces: more warheads, missile silos, delivery systems and survivability, across land, sea and air, with the goal of a stronger and more credible second-strike capability.

The key facts: 600+ warheads (January 2025 estimate), roughly 100 added per year since 2023, and about 350 new ICBM silos completed or near completion. China is the world's fastest-growing nuclear arsenal.

Why China is expanding: to deter the United States more credibly, to strengthen second-strike capability, to protect great-power status, and to respond to missile defence and strategic rivalry.

On doctrine: China officially maintains a "No First Use" policy, but an expanding arsenal raises questions about posture and transparency. Doctrine matters, yet force structure also shapes perceptions, and Taiwan-related crises could complicate signalling.

The emerging three-power order. Global nuclear politics is no longer purely US-Russia bilateral. China is now too important to ignore in future arms control, the US must think about Russia and China together, and deterrence is becoming triangular and more complex. That makes it more dangerous: harder arms control with no easy numerical balance, multiple flashpoints in Europe, the Indo-Pacific and Taiwan, three-way arms-race pressure, and crises that can interconnect across regions.

The South Asia chain reaction, which is how this topic reaches Pakistan. China's rise shapes India's nuclear modernization, India's response shifts Pakistan's threat perception, and the result is the China-India-Pakistan nuclear triangle. Pakistan can also be affected indirectly by US-China rivalry. The takeaway: China's expansion is turning deterrence, arms control and crisis management into a three-power problem.

Reference

📖 Glossary

Nuclear modernization
Upgrading arsenals rather than merely enlarging them: missiles, submarines, bombers, warheads, command and control, early warning and cyber resilience.
Nuclear triad
Delivery from land, sea and air, the structure the US and others modernise to keep deterrence survivable.
Second-strike capability
The ability to retaliate after absorbing a first strike. Everything destabilising in this class works by undermining confidence in it.
MIRV
Multiple Independently Targetable Re-entry Vehicle: one missile carrying several warheads aimed at different targets.
Hypersonic weapons
Very high-speed delivery systems that are hard to track and intercept, cutting warning time and raising crisis ambiguity.
Missile defence
Systems to detect, track, intercept or destroy incoming missiles; defensive in design but destabilising in effect.
JCPOA
The 2015 Iran nuclear deal. The US withdrew in 2018 and Iran then reduced compliance.
IAEA safeguards
Inspection and verification measures ensuring nuclear material is used peacefully; they depend on access, declarations and cooperation.
HEU / 60% enrichment
Highly enriched uranium. 60% is far above civilian fuel need and much closer to weapons-grade.
E3
The UK, France and Germany, who support diplomacy with Iran but demand transparency.
Extended deterrence
A nuclear-armed state extending protection to allies, as with the US nuclear umbrella over NATO Europe.
NATO nuclear sharing
US weapons forward-deployed in Europe with allied participation in planning and delivery; about 100 B61 bombs by independent estimates.
Nuclear ambiguity (opacity)
Neither confirming nor denying possession, as Israel does, to keep deterrence while avoiding declaration.
MENWFZ
Middle East Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone, a longstanding regional goal blocked by the disarmament-first versus peace-first dispute.
Limited war
Restricted conflict between nuclear-armed states, bounded in objectives, geography, duration and weapons, fought below the nuclear threshold.
Stability-instability paradox
Nuclear weapons deter total war but thereby open space for limited or sub-conventional conflict.
Full-spectrum deterrence
Pakistan's posture, intended to deter even limited aggression and so deny India space for limited war.
No First Use (NFU)
A declared policy not to use nuclear weapons first. China officially maintains it.
Three-power nuclear order
Deterrence and arms control becoming a US-Russia-China problem rather than a bilateral one.
Security dilemma
One state's defensive measure reads as threatening to another, driving the spiral. NATO calls sharing deterrence; Russia calls it provocation.
Self-test

🎴 Flashcards

Define nuclear modernization.

Upgrading arsenals rather than just increasing numbers: missiles, submarines, bombers, warheads, command and control, early warning, cyber resilience and space surveillance, aimed at credibility, survivability, speed and precision.

Give the four drivers of the new arms race.

Great-power rivalry (US, Russia, China), weak arms control, new technologies (AI, cyber, missile defence, MIRVs, hypersonics), and regional rivalries.

Quote the global nuclear numbers (Jan 2025).

12,241 total warheads; 9,614 in military stockpiles; 3,912 deployed; all 9 nuclear states modernising; over $100bn spent in 2024, up ~11%. Sources: SIPRI Yearbook 2025, FAS, ICAN.

Contrast India's and Pakistan's modernization posture.

India modernises against both Pakistan and China; Pakistan is India-centric with slow, steady modernization and system improvement.

What is the key takeaway on modernization?

The danger lies not only in the number of weapons but in rapid modernization, weak arms control and rising mistrust.

Give the Iran nuclear timeline.

2015 JCPOA agreed; 2018 US withdraws; 2019-2021 Iran gradually reduces compliance; 2026 IAEA monitoring and diplomatic tension remain central.

Why does 60% enrichment matter?

It is far above civilian fuel levels and much closer to weapons-grade; the E3 note Iran is the only non-nuclear-weapon state producing 60% HEU. The real issue is intention and verification.

What are IAEA safeguards and what do they depend on?

Inspection and verification measures ensuring peaceful use. They depend on access, declarations and cooperation; without them diplomacy loses credibility.

Name the four possible outcomes of the Iran crisis.

A diplomatic deal with stronger verification; a frozen crisis; military escalation; or Iran reaching threshold nuclear status.

Define MIRV and say why it destabilises.

Multiple Independently Targetable Re-entry Vehicles: one missile, several warheads on different targets. It multiplies destructive potential and complicates missile defence.

Why is missile defence destabilising if it is defensive?

It weakens an opponent's confidence in retaliation (second-strike capability), so it triggers action-reaction cycles and arms racing.

What do hypersonics change?

They are hard to track and intercept, cut warning time, increase crisis ambiguity and raise escalation pressure.

Define extended deterrence and NATO nuclear sharing.

Extended deterrence is a nuclear state protecting allies (the US umbrella over Europe). Nuclear sharing forward-deploys US weapons in Europe with allied planning and delivery roles; about 100 B61 bombs by independent estimates.

Give two criticisms of NATO nuclear sharing.

It keeps nuclear weapons politically relevant and weakens disarmament norms; critics see it as conflicting with the spirit of the NPT.

Why is NATO sharing useful to a Pakistani candidate?

It shows nuclear weapons tied to alliances and credibility, highlights double standards, and contrasts with South Asia's self-reliant model: Pakistan has no umbrella and maintains its own deterrent.

Define Israel's nuclear ambiguity and its purpose.

Neither confirming nor denying nuclear weapons, linked to the formula of not being first to introduce them in the Middle East. Purpose: maintain uncertainty while preserving deterrence.

What is the double-standards argument on Israel?

Israel is outside the NPT, and critics contrast strict scrutiny of Iran with silence over Israel's undeclared capability.

What blocks a Middle East Nuclear-Weapon-Free Zone?

Arab states and Iran want Israel's status addressed; Israel argues peace and security guarantees must come first. The dispute is disarmament-first versus peace-first.

Define limited war under nuclear conditions.

Restricted conflict between nuclear-armed states, limited in objectives, geography, duration and weapons, fought below the nuclear threshold while avoiding all-out war.

Explain the stability-instability paradox.

Nuclear weapons may prevent total war but create space for limited or sub-conventional conflict: higher-level stability produces lower-level instability.

Contrast India's and Pakistan's approach to limited war.

India seeks punitive but controlled responses and room below Pakistan's nuclear threshold. Pakistan denies that space through full-spectrum deterrence and signals escalation may not stay controllable.

List the South Asian crisis sequence.

Kargil 1999, Parliament crisis 2001-02, Mumbai 2008, Uri 2016, Balakot 2019, and the May 2025 crisis.

State the central danger in South Asia.

Not necessarily deliberate nuclear war, but the belief that a limited conventional conflict can be controlled, until events, perceptions and pressures push escalation further.

Why does Gulf nuclear diplomacy matter to Pakistan?

Iran is a neighbour; Gulf stability underpins remittances, trade and energy security; Hormuz tension raises oil prices; and Pakistan must balance Iran against Gulf Arab partners.

What is Pakistan's best approach in the Gulf?

Support de-escalation and dialogue, back peaceful nuclear use under safeguards, keep balanced diplomacy with Iran, Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Qatar, present Pakistan as a responsible nuclear state, and avoid becoming party to Gulf rivalries.

What is the UAE model?

The regional example of peaceful nuclear energy where strong safeguards and transparency built international confidence.

Give the key facts on China's expansion.

600+ warheads (Jan 2025), about 100 added per year since 2023, and roughly 350 new ICBM silos. It is the world's fastest-growing arsenal.

What is China's declared doctrine, and the tension in it?

Officially No First Use, but the expanding arsenal raises questions about posture and transparency: doctrine matters, yet force structure shapes perceptions.

Explain the three-power nuclear order.

Nuclear politics is no longer US-Russia bilateral. China is too important to exclude from arms control, so deterrence becomes triangular, with multiple flashpoints and interconnected crises.

How does China's expansion reach Pakistan?

China's rise shapes India's modernization, India's response shifts Pakistan's threat perception, creating the China-India-Pakistan nuclear triangle.

Before the paper

🎯 Exam focus

  • Always land on Pakistan. Every infographic in this class ends with a Pakistan or CSS relevance panel, and so should your answer. Global analysis without the Pakistani implication reads as a textbook summary.
  • Lead with the framing sentence: the danger today is not the number of weapons but rapid modernization, weak arms control and rising mistrust. It works as an opener for almost any nuclear question.
  • Quantify. 12,241 warheads, 9,614 stockpiled, 3,912 deployed, 9 modernising states, $100bn+ in 2024, China 600+ and adding ~100 a year with ~350 new silos. Attribute to SIPRI, FAS or ICAN.
  • Master the stability-instability paradox. It is the single most examinable concept here and it explains why a nuclear South Asia still sees Kargil, Uri and Balakot. Pair it always with India's search for space and Pakistan's full-spectrum deterrence answer.
  • Use the crisis timeline as evidence, not decoration: Kargil 1999, Parliament 2001-02, Mumbai 2008, Uri 2016, Balakot 2019, May 2025. It proves the paradox is operating in practice.
  • The double-standards line is yours to make. NATO nuclear sharing and Israeli ambiguity together give a strong, evidence-based case on non-discriminatory nuclear governance without resorting to rhetoric.
  • Explain why the new technologies destabilise rather than just naming them. The mechanism in each case is the same: they erode confidence in second-strike capability and compress decision time.
  • For Iran, separate technology from intent. The examinable point is that verification and political intention, not centrifuges alone, decide the outcome. Close with diplomacy plus credible verification.
  • China is a structural argument, not a country study. Its value is that it makes deterrence triangular and drives the China-India-Pakistan chain reaction.
